0:99285.title: No Surprises Act (Effective January 1, 2022)
0:99285.citation: Emergency services: Patients cannot be balance-billed for emergency care at any facility, in-network or out-of-network.
0:99285.why_it_applies: This is an emergency department visit at Princeton General Hospital, and the No Surprises Act explicitly prohibits balance billing for emergency services regardless of network status, meaning the patient owes only their in-network cost-sharing amount under their BlueCross PPO plan.

0:99285.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
0:99285.citation: Use CareGap's CMS PFS pricing tool to compare each CPT code's charge against the Medicare-allowed amount. A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
0:99285.why_it_applies: At 16.7x the Medicare-allowed rate, this charge far exceeds the 3-5x threshold flagged as potentially excessive in the dispute guidance, giving the patient strong grounds to formally dispute CPT 99285 with the hospital billing department and, if unresolved, file a grievance with BlueCross PPO or the State Insurance Commissioner.

1:71046.title: No Surprises Act (Effective January 1, 2022)
1:71046.citation: Emergency services: Patients cannot be balance-billed for emergency care at any facility, in-network or out-of-network.
1:71046.why_it_applies: This service was rendered in an emergency department, so the No Surprises Act applies; the patient can only be charged their in-network cost-sharing amount regardless of any out-of-network billing arrangements, and an excessive facility charge may violate this protection.

1:71046.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
1:71046.citation: A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
1:71046.why_it_applies: At 12.8x the Medicare rate, this charge far exceeds the 3-5x threshold flagged as potentially excessive in the dispute guidance, giving the patient clear grounds to formally dispute CPT 71046 through the provider's billing department and, if unresolved, through their insurer or the State Attorney General.

2:93010.title: No Surprises Act (Effective January 1, 2022)
2:93010.citation: Emergency services: Patients cannot be balance-billed for emergency care at any facility, in-network or out-of-network.
2:93010.why_it_applies: This ECG was performed in an emergency department setting, meaning the No Surprises Act applies and the patient's liability should be limited to their in-network cost-sharing amount, regardless of any out-of-network billing arrangements.

2:93010.title: Common Billing Errors to Check
2:93010.citation: A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
2:93010.why_it_applies: The ECG is billed at $310.00 against a Medicare rate of $66.56, representing a 4.7x multiplier that falls squarely within the knowledge base's flagged range of 3-5x or more, indicating a potential overcharge worth disputing.

2:93010.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
2:93010.citation: Use CareGap's CMS PFS pricing tool to compare each CPT code's charge against the Medicare-allowed amount. A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
2:93010.why_it_applies: The patient should request an itemized bill, compare CPT 93010 against the CMS Physician Fee Schedule rate of $66.56, and formally dispute the $310.00 charge by contacting the billing department and referencing the excessive multiplier.

letter.subject: Formal Dispute of Excessive Charges — Account #931058 — Michael K. Patel — Date of Service 02/28/2024
letter.body:
Date: [Today's Date]

[Your Address]
[City, State, ZIP]
[Your Phone Number]
[Your Email Address]

To:
Princeton General Hospital — Emergency Department
Billing Department
500 University Blvd
Princeton, NJ 08540

Re: Formal Billing Dispute
Patient Name: Michael K. Patel
Date of Service: February 28, 2024
Account Number: 931058
Insurance: BlueCross PPO
Total Amount Billed: $5,110.00

Dear Billing Department,

I am writing on behalf of Michael K. Patel to formally dispute charges on the above-referenced account. A review of the itemized bill for services rendered on February 28, 2024 has identified multiple line items billed at rates that appear significantly disproportionate to established Medicare-allowed amounts as published in the CMS Physician Fee Schedule. We respectfully request a thorough review and correction of the charges identified below.

DISPUTED CHARGES

1. CPT 99285 — Emergency Department Visit, Level 5 (High Severity)
Amount Billed: $2,850.00
Medicare-Allowed Amount: $170.73
Multiplier: 16.7x the Medicare rate
This charge is approximately sixteen and a half times the Medicare-allowed amount for the same service. A charge at this level is not consistent with customary billing practices and warrants immediate review and justification.

2. CPT 71046 — Chest X-Ray, 2 Views
Amount Billed: $425.00
Medicare-Allowed Amount: $33.28
Multiplier: 12.8x the Medicare rate
This charge is approximately twelve and a half times the Medicare-allowed amount for the same service. This level of markup is excessive and requires a detailed explanation or correction.

3. CPT 93010 — Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)
Amount Billed: $310.00
Medicare-Allowed Amount: $66.56
Multiplier: 4.7x the Medicare rate
This charge is approximately four and a half times the Medicare-allowed amount for the same service. Industry guidance consistently identifies charges exceeding three to five times the Medicare rate as potentially excessive.

APPLICABLE LEGAL PROTECTIONS

Please be advised that the following legal protections apply to this account.

Under the No Surprises Act, effective January 1, 2022, patients receiving emergency services cannot be balance-billed for emergency care at any facility, whether in-network or out-of-network. This protection applies to the emergency department services billed under this account. Any charges that exceed the applicable in-network cost-sharing amounts and that are passed to the patient in excess of those limits may constitute a violation of this federal law.

Further, billing guidance from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services indicates that charges significantly above the Medicare-allowed rate — generally defined as three to five times or more — may be considered excessive. All three line items identified above meet or exceed this threshold.

REQUEST FOR ITEMIZED RECORDS

Pursuant to HIPAA Section 164.524, we formally request a complete itemized statement of all charges associated with account number 931058 for the date of service February 28, 2024. This statement should include, at minimum, each CPT or procedure code billed, a plain-language description of each service, the date each service was rendered, the name of the ordering or treating provider, and the amount charged for each individual line item.

Please provide this documentation within thirty (30) days of the date of this letter.

REQUESTED RESOLUTION

We request that Princeton General Hospital conduct a full audit of the charges on this account and provide one or more of the following: a written explanation and clinical justification for each charge that exceeds five times the applicable Medicare-allowed amount; a corrected bill reflecting charges that are consistent with reasonable and customary rates; or a written statement confirming that no balance beyond the applicable in-network cost-sharing amount will be pursued from the patient, in accordance with the No Surprises Act.

DEADLINE AND NEXT STEPS

Please respond in writing no later than thirty (30) days from the date of this letter. If we do not receive a satisfactory response within that timeframe, we will have no choice but to escalate this matter by filing formal complaints with the New Jersey Office of the Attorney General, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), and the New Jersey Department of Banking and Insurance. We may also pursue resolution through the independent dispute resolution process established under the No Surprises Act.

We prefer to resolve this matter directly with your billing department and appreciate your prompt attention.

Sincerely,

[Your Name]
[Your Title or Relationship to Patient, if applicable]
[Your Address]
[Your Phone Number]
[Your Email Address]

Enclosures: Copy of itemized bill received, Copy of insurance card (front and back)
letter.recipient_block:
To:
Princeton General Hospital — Emergency Department
Billing Department
500 University Blvd
Princeton, NJ 08540

roadmap.title: Request Itemized Bill with CPT Codes
roadmap.detail: Contact Princeton General Hospital's billing department and request a fully itemized bill listing every charge with its CPT code, date of service, and unit price. You are legally entitled to this document and it is the foundation for disputing the 3 flagged charges.

roadmap.title: Review Flagged Charges Against Benchmark Rates
roadmap.detail: CareGap has already identified $3,314.43 in potential savings across 3 flagged charges. Cross-reference each flagged line item on your itemized bill against the CPT codes and estimated fair prices provided in your CareGap report to confirm the discrepancies before writing your dispute letter.

roadmap.title: Assert Your No Surprises Act Protections
roadmap.detail: Because this is an emergency bill, you are protected under the No Surprises Act (effective January 1, 2022), which limits out-of-network cost-sharing to in-network rates for emergency services. Notify the hospital in writing that you are invoking these protections and request that all charges be recalculated at the applicable in-network rate.

roadmap.title: Send a Formal Dispute Letter to the Billing Department
roadmap.detail: Draft and send a certified dispute letter to Princeton General Hospital's billing department citing the specific flagged charges, the No Surprises Act, and any identified billing errors such as duplicate charges, upcoding, or unbundling. Request a written response and ask that collections activity be paused while the dispute is under review.

roadmap.title: File a Complaint with Your Insurer and State Authorities
roadmap.detail: If the hospital does not resolve the dispute within 30 days, file a formal grievance with your health insurance company and simultaneously submit a complaint to your state's Insurance Commissioner or Attorney General, referencing the No Surprises Act violation. Given the $5,110 total and $3,314.43 in disputed charges, regulators treat this amount as a priority case.

roadmap.title: Engage a Healthcare Billing Advocate or Attorney
roadmap.detail: Given that the disputed amount of $3,314.43 is substantial, consult a certified medical billing advocate or a healthcare attorney if the complaint process does not yield a resolution. Many advocates work on contingency or a percentage of savings, and an attorney can pursue legal remedies including No Surprises Act arbitration.

summary:
# Your Hospital Bill Summary

The hospital charged you $2,850 for your emergency room visit, but Medicare says that same service should only cost about $171—that's a difference of roughly $2,679 that looks like it might be a mistake. Overall, the hospital bill has about $3,314 in charges that seem way too high compared to what insurance companies typically pay for these services. You should definitely contact the hospital's billing department to ask why these charges are so much higher than the standard rates.
